speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape nine. We are interviewing Dr. William Perl. You were telling me about setting up a fake consulate. And you recommended to others that they do the same thing. Do you know where else they set up fake--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did-- I know Paris, and I know in-- in-- I think in Warsaw, too, I think, there is. In Berlin there was one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they did it much better than I did. They didn't-- they didn't have a office there. They didn't have a sign there. They just had the stamp, and there all of us they gave the stamps. They didn't need all this. But in my-- my personality, I asked for the big show. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was much simpler, and it cost less money.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before, I had asked you about this experience you had, as His Excellency, the Consul from-- we had gotten, in your story, as far as spring of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you pick up the story? What happened to you in the spring of 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in jail in Athens. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was to be deported to Germany, to be handed over to the Germans. I had a document where the British Naval agents-- Naval Intelligence-- informs-- no, the-- no, the British ambassador in Athens informs the foreign secretary in London that the US Naval Intelligence informs him that I am already deported from Athens. And I was to be deported to Germany. I knew that. And, on the border-- you see, Greece doesn't border Germany. In between, you have to go through Yugoslav's territory.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- when we came to Idomene-- I-D-O-M-E-N-E-- Idomene, which is this small border station between Greece and Yugoslavia. I knew, now, when I am in Yugoslavia, the next thing is the border, and the Yugoslavs have to get rid of me. They didn't want me. And the next thing, he says, that they give me, hand me over to the German-- Austrian-- Austrian-- German authorities. So I decided I will not-- I decided before, I will not fall into Eichmann's hands. Eichmann had threatened me, you know that? Once, in Bucharest, I called our office. There was some trouble with getting files, or getting the visa out, and so I don't remember what the trouble was. And Eichmann was just there. And he came to the phone, and said, Perl, get the people out. I said, I'm trying my best. I don't remember the words, but that was the sense of the conversation. He said, if you have to come here, we'll give you [SPEAKING GERMAN] means free-- free access, and can-- you will not be bothered by the authorities. You can leave again.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, thank you, Mr. Eichmann, but I'm in a free country now. I was in Romania then. I was in a country-- I mean, a free country, and I do not intend to come to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was nothing offensive, but he was furious about it. He says, we will still get you, Perl. And hung up.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I certainly didn't have any intention to get into Eichmann's hands. And I went to the bathroom, to a little toilet in a [INAUDIBLE] Greek wagon, . But it was not a very big one, because it was a good train. And sit down there, and I had prepared for this. I had-- in my jacket, I had razor blades. At this time, the people used to shave with this [INAUDIBLE]. I took the razor blade, and I cut myself. Until short time ago, you could still see it. That's OK. So you slit your wrist?

speaker: interviewee
text: I slit my wrist, and the blood came out. And after a few minutes, I got dizzy. And I saw the blood on the floor, but I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] still thought, what will become of Lore? Will Lore ever learn what happened to me? And then I passed out. I don't remember that part. I remember the next thing I knew was that a man was putting bandages around me. There was a Yugoslav physician on the train. And he bandaged me. And the Greeks didn't let me enter. They also didn't force me to go into Yugoslavia. Yugoslavia wouldn't have taken me, anyhow, in this condition. And I was brought to the home of a farmer who lived on the border between the two countries. He was a Greek. And I was recuperating there. I didn't know how weak I was. I was very weak. But I was given milk, very much milk, and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] turtles. They have turtles there. They run around, the turtles, by the hundreds. And I got turtle, and turtle soup, and turtle meat, which is a main in this town there. In the meanwhile, the Irgun had learned what has happened to me. I don't remember how I did it. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this Mrs. Bernstein came. And somehow, yes, the Irgun came and spoke to the-- spoke to the Chief Rabbi of Saloniki, that he should-- Jews were very influential in Greece. There were-- was a very powerful Jewish community in Saloniki. I think are the largest percentage of any city in Europe of Jews. And the Chief Rabbi said, to this criminal, who breaks the law, blatantly and brings shame upon all the Jews, no, I won't move a finger. The same rabbi, to my very great regret, was a year, or two years later, deported, with all his community, to Auschwitz. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now, I got back again from the border, back again, to Piraeus to the jail there. I was not in a real jail in Piraeus. I was-- there was part of a staircase, which led to the outside, which was locked, and was not used. And there was a door-- I was living on a staircase. But it was much better than a jail. And there was [INAUDIBLE], and after two or three days, a monk came to me-- Father Richard Pliebel. Father Richard, who had been sent by the Irgun, who had somehow established-- there was a Jew with the name of [INAUDIBLE], who had connections with the Irgun, I suppose. But it had something with smuggling weapons. This [INAUDIBLE] probably did. I don't know. But he was a [SPEAKING GERMAN] baptized Jew-- Catholic Jew. And he worked for me, for to getting me a visa. It was-- my passport had expired, in the meanwhile. And he got me a visa. He was the-- he was the Father Confessor of the German Embassy. So they got the right man. And first of all, I got an extension of the visa by the German Embassy. And then he got me from a Catholic country, from Portugal, a visa, which was not a real visa from the consulate, but which he told me was arranged by somebody in a coffee house. They arranged for the visa. But he knew that this guy forges Portuguese visas. So I got a Portuguese visa. And now, with a Portuguese visa, and my British visa, I took a [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] fly ticket with with [INAUDIBLE] to Portugal, which was good. It was a very good country, very few refugees there, and was not forcing out refugees. I was, I didn't write about this in my book. I was horrified when we first come to Brindisi. The plane landed, and they are still small planes at this time. In Brindisi, and then-- then in Rome, and then continued to Lisbon-- to Madrid, and to Lisbon. And in Brindisi-- before he landed in Brindisi, he turned to-- no, after he had passed Brindisi-- actually, he didn't have to land in Brindisi. We go straight to Rome. He turned around, and I noticed that he turned around, [INAUDIBLE]. But there was some engine trouble. And he landed in Brindisi, not on the schedule. And we went, then, onto Rome.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Rome, of course, I-- I was-- I had a transit visa, but I had been arrested in Rome before, for overstaying in Italy. So I went again to a good hotel, the best hotel in Rome, the Excelsior. And stayed there all night, and the next morning, the familiar police came to arrest me. And I told them, you have to deport me? Germany's the only country which will take you. They were sorry. You are here without permission. You have overstayed your permission. You abuse our hospitality. Germany's the only country. We have to send you to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, this is my order, to brief you, to explain this for Germany. I said, this is crazy. I have a ticket to Portugal, where I can start a new life. The Italians were very understanding. They didn't bring me to a plane for Germany. The plane [INAUDIBLE] plane goes to Portugal. There was excitement every day. And the plane landed in Madrid, intermittently. And in Madrid, this Franco regime was actually not against the Jews. But the people knew that the Jews are communists. They were supposed to be communists. They had propaganda about it. And the man came. The passport control came on board, and said, you have entered Spain without permission. I have to arrest you. I said, I didn't have to enter. I wanted to fly straight over Spain to Portugal. He says, I'm sorry. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I talked my way out. So he let we continue to Portugal. And in Portugal, where I arrived, the Portugal authorities said, this visa may not valid. We have to deport you tomorrow morning. We have a plane going-- leaving for Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I faced, now, what has helped me before-- the real thing-- at first I slit my wrist again, but not deep enough to die for me. And so they brought me to the hospital in Lisbon. And in the meanwhile, I got-- I got-- I got the permission to stay-- to stay in Portugal for, I think, two months or so. In the meanwhile, the war had really broken out. The war had broken out on the first of September, but there was no fighting except Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Western Front, on the 10th of May, 19-- 1940, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Germans invaded Holland and Belgium and France. And now many, many refugees came over to Portugal from France. So I didn't wait for these two months to be over. Of course, the situation was very difficult. And I learned from one of the refugees that you can get a visa to Shanghai.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went, and I got-- I don't think I went myself. He got it for me. I got a visa to Shanghai. Now, how do you get to Shanghai from Portugal? I inquire. There's no ships going to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, first of all, I wanted from Portugal, still to continue the organization of the trips. Maybe [INAUDIBLE] boat and bring the people who were around Portugal out. And with their money, the people who were still-- add to them the people who are still in Germany. But while I was in a-- in a central drugstore, looking for a pair of sunglasses, [INAUDIBLE] turn these things, there was a man standing next to me. And he told me, as he was standing next to me, very inconspicuously, Mr. Perl. If you show yourself again in the port-- because I'd been in the port before, to find out-- in the port just once more, I guarantee you, you will be put on the next plane, and we will see to it that it's a plane which brings you back to Germany. One more move. He didn't repeat. That's all he said.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew I couldn't go into the port anymore, and I had to give up my plan to do something here. And that's when I went for the Chinese visa for Shanghai. And there was no boat. So I had to take a ship to Mozambique. To Lourenco Marques, which is on the other side, on the southeast side of Portugal. And I went with the ship. Now, to get this ship I needed a Portuguese exit visa. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I thought that I getting the permission to stay in-- to Mozambique. But it was an exit visa to Mozambique, not a visa from Mozambique.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Mozambique, it was a very interesting trip. It was a freighter, which has about 20 passengers, too. We landed on many small ports, Saint-Omer, and in [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Angola, in this-- so there were some other British, Portuguese colonies there, which I don't remember. We went to Cape Town, but I couldn't leave the boat in Cape Town But I could see the beautiful city and the mountains there, with the flat top. And we came to Mozambique. Now, in Mozambique, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was suppose to wait for a ship that was to Shanghai. It was very difficult, but they said there will be ships with your part of it, maybe to Singapore, and from Singapore you can go to Shanghai. But I knew that the Shanghai visa was worth nothing. You could come on the land way to Shanghai, but only from the sea, you couldn't. You had to go through-- The people in Shanghai who-- the people who had issued me the visa for Shanghai were not the people in power anymore in China. And they couldn't do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, fortunately, the consul, the American consul to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Laurenco Marques was a very friendly man, very good man, and we ate in the same restaurant before. I had met an acquaintance there. And next day I came, and he asked me what my plans are. I said, I would like to get into the United States. On the ship, on the-- [INAUDIBLE] was the name of the ship-- I had met Mr.-- I forgot the name. Maybe it comes to my mind-- who was a very, very well known farmer-- a big farmer-- in Israel, in Palestine, and very, very known, politically, quite an important figure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told me, you will never get to Palestine. I wanted to go to Palestine. You have to go around the East Coast of Africa, everywhere the British are the pwer. If you go on the land here, if you have any-- you can't get there without getting through Egype. And Egypt is the headquarters of the British Intelligence for the Mideast. You'll never get there. They'll take you, and so, and if you would get there, you would-- [INAUDIBLE] I would be only trouble for the Irgun, because I would be arrested, and they would have to take me out. There was no chance to get to Palestine.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to the consulate next morning and asked him for a visa to America. This was crazy. There was-- America was the hardest country to get into. And he was enthused and started selling me America. [INAUDIBLE] He says, you are bright young man, and you will make your way in America. I'm very happy that you will go to America. Our country will profit from you, and you will profit from the country [INAUDIBLE] visa.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to him, I do not-- don't know anything about a visa. I do with the ships which [INAUDIBLE] She-- there was a woman there, sharp nose, very sharp woman. She will take care of it. Come back tomorrow morning. She didn't like it at all. He says, I could issue the visa now, but she's gone already. Next morning, I came back, and she knew about it. She said we can't give you a visa [INAUDIBLE] because you-- you need a quota number for [INAUDIBLE] and you need an affidavit.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went back to him, to the man, and I said, can't you get me a quota number? He says, I can find out. And he called the embassy in Pretoria, or the consulate in Cape Town, and he found out they have quota numbers for a Czech to emigrate into Palestine-- into America. Imagine-- do you see how the State Department tried to keep people out for a price. You had to be born-- you went by the place of your birth. What person born in Czechoslovakia will apply at the consulate in Johannesburg-- that's what it was-- in Johannesburg to go into America? That's a quota number. And they said that you need an affidavit, too. I said, who has to give the affidavit? Says, any American citizen that is substantial, this is very important. I said, you are substantial. You can give-- [INAUDIBLE] you can give me the affidavit. He was taken aback for a moment. Then he said, you are right. And he was ready to give me the affidavit. But this works very much against the purposes of this woman. She said, with the affidavit, and with the quota number, he'll have to wait years before he gets into America now. He can't get it. If you really want to get him into America, he has to get another kind of visa. He says, what other kinds of visa are? Says visitor's visa and a transit visa, she said. OK. I'll give you a visitor's visa. I said, fine. They says, but this is only for a limited time. You will learn the right way to make it permanent. I said, fine. And why didn't the visitor's visa come up? Yeah, she told him you can't give him a visitor visa. A visitor must have the possibility to return to own country. I said, that's no problem. Give me a transit visa. I'll have that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, i have a-- I have a visa for Venezuela. This Venezuela visa was definitely bought in a coffee house in Athens. Says, no, that's good. So they give me a visa, the American transit visa, for transit to Venezuela. He said, but within 30 or 60 days you will have to leave the United States. I said, that's no problem. I knew once I'm in America, I wouldn't leave any more.

speaker: interviewee
text: So what was next? And next, a ship came. And this ship, which was going to America. And I could have gone with this ship, and I didn't want to. I was sitting safe there now. And I figured the ship would [INAUDIBLE] have trouble with his visa, because the Venezuela visa didn't work. I am a Flying Dutchman. I have to wait for an American ship, which will [INAUDIBLE] me to America. A week later, an American ship came. It was the SS Mary of the Blue Line domiciled in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I bought tickets in this agency. As there was another Czech Jew who was with me, [NON-ENGLISH] Who bought tickets to [INAUDIBLE] American. It was his girlfriend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the name of the American. I learned from him a lot about America on the way. And so we got these tickets. Now, when I wanted to get on the boat, I went on the day before the ship was to sail, two days before. It stayed several days in port. So if the ship has some [NON-ENGLISH], some complication, I can counteract it. And there was a complication. The Captain Broderick was drunk from the moment we left the port until we came to the coast of America. But he knew his thing. He says, I can't take you. You will become a Flying Dutchman, because there may be something wrong with this visa. There may be something wrong with the Venezuela visa. I said, you don't have to use Venezuela visa. You have a consul, [INAUDIBLE] No, I don't dare to do it, because I would be a fool in the eyes of my company if I take a refugee on board with a doubtful entry visa. He knew the business.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I asked him, listen. if you would be on the high seas, and you will see a man drowning, would you take him? And he said, yes. I said, in any case, with a destination visa or not? Yes, he said. I would take you. I am in this position. He said, how come? I said, because I've only temporary stay in Mozambique, and the Portuguese will turn me over to the Germans unless I leave within a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: He says, let me think it over. And, in the meanwhile, I went to the Portuguese police, and told them, you have to confirm that you will deport me if I am not leaving within 48 or so to help and to give me this confirmation say, you will deport me, because I am-- I'm supposed to wait for a boat to China. And there is no ship for China. So I thought him-- took me on board. And so I arrived in Baltimore. After several stops by the way, I arrived in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: In September, 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression of the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first impression was that I went to a restaurant there, delicatessen. And they had on the menu gefilte fish, which I didn't even know existed. This was a Jewish place, and I was very much impressed with gefilte fish-- gefilte fish. But before that, I had an experience on the street. I had-- I had one of those tropical hats on, and I looked rather dramatic in it, in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went-- I took a shower, and went to see a little bit of the city, to see America. And when I crossing across the street, I felt a heavy hand-- or maybe it wasn't heavy. It appeared to me heavy-- on my shoulder, and there was a policeman. I was really scared. [INAUDIBLE] And he said, Jack, and he said something which I didn't understand. And I said, I'm sorry. My name is not Jack. My name is Willie Perl. He says, whatever it is, you will kill yourself when you cross here. Don't you see this light? There were only two or three lights in Vienna at the time I left, and people waited, and observed them with interest. And it means that you should be careful when you cross. That's how we understood it. So, then he asked me, how long have you been here in this country? I said, I'm sorry. I did not know. I did not fully understand the meaning of the light. He says, how long have you been in this country? I looked at the watch, and I said, two and a half hours. OK, Jack. Watch out for yourself, else you'll get yourself killed. How many languages do you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now, I still speak English, German, Czech, French, and Italian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you spoke English in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I spoke it fairly well. Not as well as I do now. But I spoke, then, more languages. I spoke Portuguese. I spoke Greek-- Greek. And with my Czech, I understood. I could communicate with the-- with the Yugoslavs, and I understood Romanian, too. Romanian is amazingly close to Latin. And I learned Latin in school, too. And Greek, I have learned in schools [INAUDIBLE]. For many years, it was ancient Greek. But I could communicate.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you treated by the Americans 1940, when you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came? Very matter of fact. These people, were very matter of fact. They checked, and they interviewed me. And they found that I'm really going to Venezuela. I would have gone to Venezuela. If I couldn't have stayed in America, I would have been arrested there, probably, because the visa wasn't valid.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to have to pause to change tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
